chunks.text: # Asset Policy — Auto PPT
**Version:** 1.0.0 **Status:** Authoritative for v1 **Scope:** All image, chart, icon, and font assets consumed by the renderer
## 1. Purpose

The renderer is deterministic. It accepts only local, cached, integrity-verified assets at render time. This document defines what is allowed into the cache, what is forbidden, and what metadata every asset must carry before the renderer touches it.
## 2. V1 Source Policy: Local-Only, Provided Assets

V1 is **`provided_only`**. This mirrors the `ImageSourcePolicy.PROVIDED_ONLY` enum value that is already the default in `ImageTokens` (layout/schemas.py) and the `source_policy` field in `DEFAULT_STYLE_TOKENS` (pipeline.py).
The only assets that may enter the asset cache in v1 are:

| Source kind | Allowed in v1 | Notes | |---|---|---| | Local file path supplied by the caller | ✅ Yes | Must exist on disk at resolution time | | Chart spec rendered to PNG by `chart_renderer.py` | ✅ Yes | Treated as a locally-generated asset | | Stock image service (Getty, Unsplash, Pexels, etc.) | ❌ No | Reserved for v2 | | AI image generation (DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, etc.) | ❌ No | Reserved for v2+ | | Remote URL passed directly into a layout slot | ❌ Never | Hard-blocked at all pipeline stages | | Authenticated / short-lived CDN URL | ❌ Never | Hard-blocked; URLs expire before render |

The `source_policy` field on `StyleTokens.images` controls which path the asset stage takes. Callers that try to set a non-`provided_only` policy in v1 must receive a clear `ValueError` at the top of `resolve_assets`.
## 3. What Is Forbidden
The following are unconditional prohibitions regardless of pipeline stage or caller intent:

1. **Remote URLs in the PPTX.** No `http://` or `https://` string may appear in any rendered element. `extract_local_asset_path` (renderer/slide_ops.py) already enforces this; it must remain a hard guard, not a warning.

2. **Unverified file hashes.** Every asset that enters the cache must have its SHA-256 computed and stored in `AssetRecord.sha256` before the renderer receives the layout. An asset with a missing or malformed hash must be rejected.

3. **Assets outside the declared cache directory.** The resolver copies every provided asset into the artifacts `assets/` subdirectory. No element payload may reference a path outside that directory at render time.
4. **Lossy format coercion.** Chart PNGs are already written with `dpi=150`. Provided images must not be recompressed or converted during copy; they are hashed and stored as-is.

5. **Silent skip of missing assets.** If a `ResolvedElement` of kind `IMAGE` or `CHART` has no resolvable local path, the resolver must raise — it must not silently drop the element or substitute a placeholder. The current code in `_resolve_element_asset` already does this.
## 4. Required Asset Metadata

Every `AssetRecord` persisted to `AssetManifest` must carry the following fields. Fields marked **Required** must be non-null; fields marked **Optional-v1** may be `None` in v1 but must be present in the model so they can be populated in v2 without a schema break.

| Field | Type | Required | Description | |---|---|---|---| | `asset_id` | `str` | Required | Matches the `element_id` of the resolved element that owns this asset | | `source_type` | `AssetSourceType` enum | Required | One of: `local_provided`, `chart_rendered`, `stock` (v2), `ai_generated` (v2+) | | `local_path` | `str` | Required | Absolute path to the cached file inside the artifacts `assets/` directory | | `sha256` | `str` (64 hex chars) | Required | SHA-256 of the cached file, computed after copy/render | | `license` | `str \| None` | Optional-v1 | SPDX identifier or free-text usage terms (e.g. `"CC0-1.0"`, `"editorial_only"`) | | `alt_text` | `str \| None` | Optional-v1 | Accessibility description of the visual content; required for v2 | | `aspect_ratio` | `float \| None` | Optional-v1 | `width / height` of the cached file; used by v2 fill/fit logic | | `original_source` | `str \| None` | Optional-v1 | Provenance URI or local path before caching; the pre-copy origin | | `created_at` | `datetime \| None` | Optional-v1 | UTC timestamp of when the asset was written into the cache |
The `AssetRecord` model in `assets/resolver.py` currently has `asset_id`, `sha256`, `source_uri`, `license`, `attribution`, and `created_at`. The delta to reach compliance with this spec is:
Rename `uri` → `local_path` (or add `local_path` as the canonical field and deprecate `uri`)
Add `source_type: AssetSourceType` (new required enum)
Add `alt_text: str | None`
Add `aspect_ratio: float | None`
Rename or alias `source_uri` → `original_source`
These changes are additive and non-breaking if done as field additions with defaults before any rename.
## 5. Asset Source Type Enum

```python class AssetSourceType(str, Enum): LOCAL_PROVIDED = "local_provided" # Caller-supplied file; v1 only source CHART_RENDERED = "chart_rendered" # PNG produced by chart_renderer.py STOCK = "stock" # Downloaded from a licensed stock service (v2) AI_GENERATED = "ai_generated" # Produced by an image generation model (v2+) ```
The resolver must set `source_type` based on the element kind:
`ResolvedElementKind.CHART` → `AssetSourceType.CHART_RENDERED`
`ResolvedElementKind.IMAGE` → `AssetSourceType.LOCAL_PROVIDED` (v1)
## 6. Cache Directory Contract

``` <output_name>_artifacts/ └── assets/ ├── <sha256>.<ext> # All provided images, named by hash ├── <element_id>.png # Chart renders, named by element id pre-hash └── asset_manifest.json # Full AssetManifest written by pipeline.py ```
Rules:
File names for provided images must use the SHA
256 as the stem (current behavior in `_copy_local_asset`). This ensures identical files are deduplicated automatically.

Chart render files may use the element ID as the stem before hashing, then be renamed to the hash once written (or kept by element ID — either is acceptable as long as `local_path` in `AssetRecord` is the definitive reference).
The `asset_manifest.json` must be written by `pipeline.py` after `resolve_assets` returns and before `export_pptx` is called. This is already the case.
## 7. V2 Extension Points
When `source_policy` is upgraded beyond `provided_only`, the following additions are expected:
A downloader module (`assets/downloader.py`) responsible for fetching, verifying, and caching stock images. It must compute SHA
256 after download and refuse to write to cache on hash mismatch.

A `license` field population step — stock images must carry their license string before entering the manifest. Assets with `license: None` are only acceptable for `local_provided` and `chart_rendered` types.
An `alt_text` generation step (LLM or caption model) that runs after download/render and populates the field before the manifest is finalized.
An `aspect_ratio` check against the target slot dimensions from the template registry, with a fill/fit policy decision recorded on the `AssetRecord`.
## 8. Relationship to Existing Code

| Policy rule | Where enforced today | |---|---| | No remote URLs in the PPTX | `extract_local_asset_path` in renderer/slide_ops.py | | Local copy before render | `_copy_local_asset` in assets/resolver.py | | SHA-256 computed and stored | `_hash_file` + `AssetRecord.sha256` in assets/resolver.py | | Manifest written before export | `pipeline.py` lines 235–236 | | `source_policy` field on StyleTokens | `ImageTokens` in layout/schemas.py | | Missing asset raises, not skips | `_resolve_element_asset` in assets/resolver.py |
Fields not yet enforced: `source_type`, `alt_text`, `aspect_ratio`, `local_path` canonical name.

chunks.text: Codex Skills and Capabilities for Data Ingestion,

SlideSpec Generation, and PPTX Export Executive summary Codex (the OpenAI coding agent) is best understood as an orchestrator of deterministic tools—file I/O, scripts, build steps, and API calls—rather than a “presentation generator” by itself. In slide‑generation systems, Codex is typically most valuable where you need repeatable engineering work: parsing messy documents, normalizing data into JSON, validating schemas, building retrieval indexes, and rendering output (PPTX/OOXML) with reproducible tests and quality gates. Three Codex capabilities are especially relevant for agentic slide generation pipelines: Agent mode with a working directory: Codex CLI can read and modify a local repo and run commands in the selected directory, enabling end‑to‑end automation (e.g., parse → index → generate SlideSpec → render PPTX → run validators). Skills (SKILL.md) and progressive disclosure: Codex supports a “skills” authoring format with metadata-first loading and conditional inclusion of detailed instructions, references, and scripts— ideal for packaging repeatable slide‑pipeline workflows (e.g., “parse docs,” “build embeddings,” “render PPTX”).

Tool calling + Agents SDK + MCP interoperability: OpenAI’s platform documentation describes tool calling flows, and Codex can be exposed as an MCP server callable by the Agents SDK—useful when Codex is a sub-agent that performs “implementation work” for a larger slide-generation orchestrator . A robust architecture for SlideSpec‑driven PPTX generation is to treat Codex as the implementation and glue layer and to keep the model’s free‑form generation confined to strict JSON outputs validated by JSON Schema. JSON Schema is a standardized way to validate JSON instances; common validators include Ajv (JS) and python‑jsonschema. Assumptions (explicit): This report assumes you are building (or integrating into) a custom slide agent that uses a stable intermediate representation like SlideSpec and a deterministic rendering step to PPTX through OOXML-producing libraries (e.g., python‑pptx or PptxGenJS). Codex itself does not publicly document a built‑in SlideSpec format; SlideSpec is treated here as a design pattern and contract you define. What Codex is in practice for slide pipelines Codex is positioned as a coding agent you can run locally (Codex CLI) and in other product surfaces.
The CLI is documented as able to read, change, and run code in the selected directory, which is the core enabling factor for building an ingestion→PPTX automation pipeline.

Codex also supports: Models and long context: OpenAI’s Codex model documentation lists large context windows (e.g., 400,000 tokens for some Codex-optimized models) and configurable reasoning effort settings on Codex-optimized models, which matters when you are generating large JSON payloads or coordinating multi-step transformations. Configuration controls: Codex has config references and advanced settings, including the ability to run against local OSS providers in “OSS mode,” which is relevant for air-gapped or policy-constrained environments. Progressive disclosure “skills”: Skills are directories with a required SKILL.md; Codex starts with metadata and loads full instructions only when it selects the skill, helping keep context lean and workflows repeatable. For slide-generation tooling, the key insight is that Codex can act as: A pipeline engineer (writes and runs parsers, embedding/indexing scripts, PPTX renderers). A skill router (chooses and executes the correct SKILL.md-based workflow). A tool-using sub-agent inside a larger agent system via MCP/Agents SDK.

Inventory of Codex-relevant skills for agentic slide generation This section lists the “skills” you typically want Codex to execute, grouped by pipeline stage. For each, the report emphasizes: inputs/outputs, common libraries, and where schema validation gates should be applied. Document ingestion and parsing skills PDF text + layout extraction Codex can implement parsers with libraries like pdfplumber, which is designed to extract detailed PDF information and “easily extract text and tables” when the PDF is text-based (scanned PDFs typically require OCR). Word (.docx) ingestion python-docx is documented as a library for reading/creating/updating Word .docx files and exposes document core properties (metadata extraction), which is useful for provenance. HTML ingestion Codex can write JS/Python scrapers and HTML-to-text converters (assumption: you choose libraries like BeautifulSoup/Readability); this is usually paired with metadata capture (URL, retrieval time, allowed audiences). OCR for scanned PDFs/images Tesseract is an open-source OCR engine (Apache 2.0 license) and documents a CLI invocation pattern (e.g., tesseract imagename outputbase).
Codex can orchestrate OCR as a fallback when PDF extraction fails.

Semantic chunking and structure inference Unstructured documents “partition” raw files into structured elements and then “chunk” those elements into retrieval-friendly chunks for RAG. Codex can codify a consistent chunking policy to stay under embedding model limits and improve retrieval precision. Embeddings, vector DB, and RAG orchestration skills Embedding calls (local or hosted) SentenceTransformers is presented as a go-to Python module for computing embeddings and can load models for embedding text to vectors. Codex can build embedding pipelines with batch sizing, caching, and deterministic IDs. Vector index operations (local FAISS) FAISS is documented as a library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors, with Python wrappers. Codex can implement local indexes for quick prototyping or private deployments. Vector DB operations (Qdrant) Qdrant describes itself as a vector database/semantic search engine with a REST API to store/search/ manage vectors with payloads (metadata). Codex can implement upsert/search with tenant metadata filters and stored provenance.

RAG coordination: retrieve → ground → cite The core capability is: for each slide intent, retrieve top‑k chunks with stable IDs; provide those chunks to content generation; require citations in the SlideSpec. This is a design pattern (assumption), but it maps directly to Unstructured’s chunking-for-RAG framing and to vector search capabilities in FAISS/Qdrant. SlideSpec assembly, validation, layout solving, and PPTX generation skills JSON Schema validation gates Ajv supports multiple JSON Schema drafts (including 2020‑12) and generates fast validators; python‑jsonschema documents simple validate() usage. Codex can enforce schema validity before any downstream step (layout/renderer). SlideSpec assembly Codex can generate a canonical SlideSpec (your schema) from: brief → outline → retrieved evidence → slide blocks. The key is to produce JSON that always validates and never relies on “implied” fields. Layout solving Codex can implement rule-based layout solvers (grid, templates, constraints) and output a ResolvedLayout with absolute positions. This is critical if you want predictable PPTX output (assumption).

The low-level alternative is an imperative slide API like Google Slides batchUpdate (structured requests applied atomically), which illustrates what “deterministic layout instructions” look like in JSON form.

PPTX/OOXML generation Two mainstream options: python-pptx: documented as creating/reading/updating .pptx without requiring PowerPoint installed; often used for generating a PPTX from dynamic content such as a JSON payload. PptxGenJS: documented as outputting standards-compliant OOXML presentations compatible with PowerPoint/Keynote/LibreOffice/Google Slides import, and supports slide masters for branding. For correctness and interoperability, it helps to understand the PPTX packaging model. ECMA‑376 defines Office Open XML packaging and markup vocabularies, and Microsoft’s Open XML SDK docs describe the structure of PresentationML parts inside the .pptx ZIP package. Asset handling, export jobs, and governance skills Asset manifests and caching Codex can create an asset manifest (hashes, sources, licenses) and cache images locally so PPTX generation embeds assets reliably. This is a best practice to prevent “broken links” and to enable provenance audits (assumption). Export job orchestration Systems often render/export asynchronously (job status polling).

This pattern is explicit in multiple vendor APIs (outside Codex), but for Codex you implement this yourself: create job record → run renderer → upload artifact → return URL + expiry (assumption). Security / PII handling Codex can implement redaction gates and PII classification flags in JSON (assumption), and it can be configured to control what it reads and runs via repo boundaries and configuration. Codex best-practices documentation emphasizes validation and careful workflows. Tool-call wrappers, function schemas, and a Codex-driven pipeline This section provides copy‑paste‑ready tool schemas (function-call definitions) and a reference pipeline flow. The schema style aligns with OpenAI’s tool/function calling guidance: define tools, have the model emit tool calls, execute them, then feed tool outputs back into the model.

Mermaid flowchart of a Codex-driven pipeline flowchart TD A[Ingest inputs: PDFs, DOCX, HTML, tables] --> B[Parse + normalize] B --> C[OCR fallback for scanned pages] B --> D[Semantic chunking + metadata] D --> E[Embeddings batch job] E --> F[Vector index ops: upsert + filters] G[Brief + constraints] --> H[Generate outline JSON] H --> I[Retrieve chunks per slide] I --> J[Generate SlideSpec JSON + citations]

J --> K[JSON Schema validation gate] K --> L[Resolve layout (templates/constraints)] L --> M[Render PPTX (OOXML)] M --> N[Write asset manifest + provenance] N --> O[Export job status + artifact URL] K -->|fail| J M -->|fail| L Function-call schemas for common tools Below are compact JSON tool schemas designed for an agent runtime where Codex (or another orchestrator) can call tools. Each tool uses deterministic, typed IO to reduce hallucination risk (design principle). ingest

"name": "ingest", "description": "Register input sources (files/urls/tables), attach tenant metadata, and return ingestion_id plus canonical source records.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["tenant_id", "inputs"], "properties": { "tenant_id": { "type": "string"}, "project_id": { "type": "string"}, "inputs": { "type": "array", "minItems": 1, "items": { "type": "object", "required": ["input_id", "kind"], "properties": { "input_id": { "type": "string"}, "kind": { "type": "string", "enum": ["file", "url", "table", "text"] }, "path": { "type": "string"}, "url": { "type": "string"}, "mime_type": { "type": "string"}, "table": { "type": "object", "required": ["columns", "rows"], "properties": { "columns": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string"} }, "rows": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "array", "items":
"text": { "type": "string"}
"additionalProperties": false

"security": { "type": "object", "properties": { "data_classification": { "type": "string", "enum": ["public", "internal", "confidential", "restricted"] }, "pii_mode": { "type": "string", "enum": ["allow", "redact", "block"], "default": "redact"}
parse

"name": "parse", "description": "Parse an ingestion source into structured elements (text blocks, tables, figures) with metadata and bounding boxes when available.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["ingestion_id", "input_id"], "properties": { "ingestion_id": { "type": "string"}, "input_id": { "type": "string"}, "parser": { "type": "string", "enum": ["pdfplumber", "python_docx", "html_to_text", "unstructured"] }, "ocr_fallback": { "type": "boolean", "default": true}
pdfplumber is commonly used for PDF parsing and table extraction for text-based PDFs; Unstructured provides partitioning + chunking for RAG; Tesseract provides OCR fallback for scanned content. 29
embed

"name": "embed", "description": "Compute embeddings for chunks and upsert them into a vector index.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["chunks", "embedding_provider", "index"], "properties": { "chunks": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object", "required": ["chunk_id", "text"], "properties": { "chunk_id": { "type": "string"}, "text": { "type": "string"}, "metadata": { "type": "object"}
"embedding_provider": { "type": "object", "required": ["type"], "properties": { "type": { "type": "string", "enum": ["sentence_transformers", "openai_embeddings"] }, "model": { "type": "string"}
"index": { "type": "object", "required": ["type", "name"], "properties": { "type": { "type": "string", "enum": ["faiss", "qdrant"] }, "name": { "type": "string"}
SentenceTransformers computes embeddings; FAISS supports local similarity search; Qdrant is a vector DB with REST API and payload metadata. retrieve

"name": "retrieve", "description": "Retrieve top-k chunks for a query with optional metadata filters (tenant_id, doc_id, pii flags).", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["index", "query", "top_k"], "properties": { "index": { "type": "object", "required": ["type", "name"], "properties": { "type": { "type": "string", "enum": ["faiss", "qdrant"] }, "name": { "type": "string"}
"query": { "type": "string"}, "top_k": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 50}, "filters": { "type": "object"}
generate_outline, generate_slidespec These are “LLM steps” in your pipeline; Codex can either (a) produce JSON directly, or (b) call an LLM API and then run schema validation.

"name": "generate_outline", "description": "Generate an outline (slide goals, template keys, evidence needs) from a brief and constraints.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["brief", "template_catalog"], "properties": { "brief": { "type": "object"}, "template_catalog": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "string"} }, "slide_count_target": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 50}

"name": "generate_slidespec", "description": "Generate SlideSpec JSON (strict schema) using outline + retrieved chunks; must include citations for claims.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["outline", "retrieved_chunks_by_slide", "slidespec_schema"], "properties": { "outline": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object"} }, "retrieved_chunks_by_slide": { "type": "object"}, "slidespec_schema": { "type": "object", "description": "JSON Schema to validate output."}, "constraints": { "type": "object"}
Tool calling flows are documented in OpenAI’s function/tool calling guide, supporting multi-step “tool call → execute → tool output → continue.” resolve_layout, render_pptx, export_job

"name": "resolve_layout", "description": "Convert SlideSpec into absolute geometry (ResolvedLayout) using templates/constraints; produce QA diagnostics.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["slidespec", "layout_policy"], "properties": { "slidespec": { "type": "object"}, "layout_policy": { "type": "string", "enum": ["template_strict", "template_flexible", "freeform_grid"] }
"name": "render_pptx",

"description": "Render ResolvedLayout to a PPTX using python-pptx or PptxGenJS and embed assets.", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["resolved_layout", "renderer", "output_path"], "properties": { "resolved_layout": { "type": "object"}, "renderer": { "type": "string", "enum": ["python_pptx", "pptxgenjs", "openxml_sdk"] }, "output_path": { "type": "string"}, "asset_manifest": { "type": "object"}

"name": "export_job", "description": "Create an export job record and return status + artifact URLs (optionally expiring).", "parameters": { "type": "object", "required": ["kind", "artifact_path"], "properties": { "kind": { "type": "string", "enum": ["pptx", "pdf"] }, "artifact_path": { "type": "string"}, "expires_in_seconds": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 60}

python-pptx and PptxGenJS both produce .pptx without requiring PowerPoint installed; PptxGenJS documents OOXML compatibility and slide masters, while python‑pptx documents typical JSON/dynamic content usage. For low-level OOXML correctness and debugging, Microsoft’s Open XML SDK documentation explains PPTX structure and parts. Copy‑paste prompt templates and compact code snippets by skill This section gives Codex-ready prompts and minimal snippets. Prompts are designed to reduce hallucinations by: explicit IO contracts, library pinning, test expectations, and schema validation gates— patterns consistent with Codex “best practices” guidance emphasizing planning, validation, and using scripts deterministically.

Parsing: PDF, DOCX, HTML, OCR Prompt template (Codex): “Build a PDF parser with table extraction + OCR fallback” You are working in this repo. Create src/parse/pdf_parse.py with a function parse_pdf(path) -> NormalizedDoc.
Requirements:
Use pdfplumber to extract text per page and attempt extract_tables() for tables.
If the page has no text, render the page to an image and OCR it using Tesseract CLI
( tesseract).
Return a JSON-serializable dict: {doc_id, pages:[{page, text, tables:[...]}]}.
Add tests/test_pdf_parse.py with one synthetic fixture and a smoke test.
Print a short QA report: pages with OCR, tables found.

If anything fails, raise a typed exception with context. pdfplumber is positioned to extract text and tables from PDFs; scanned PDFs require OCR, and Tesseract documents CLI invocation. Python snippet (conceptual) importsubprocess importpdfplumber defocr_image_to_text(img_path: str) ->str: # tesseract imagename outputbase -> outputbase.txt outbase= img_path.rsplit(".", 1)[0] subprocess.run(["tesseract", img_path, outbase],check=True) withopen(outbase+ ".txt", "r", encoding="utf-8") asf: returnf.read() defparse_pdf(path: str) ->dict: doc= {"doc_id": path, "pages": []} withpdfplumber.open(path) aspdf: fori, pageinenumerate(pdf.pages, start=1): text= page.extract_text()or"" tables= page.extract_tables()or[] doc["pages"].append({"page": i, "text": text, "tables": tables}) returndoc pdfplumber’s GitHub readme describes detailed PDF extraction and table extraction; Tesseract docs cover CLI OCR. Prompt template (Codex): “Parse DOCX to structured headings + metadata”

Create src/parse/docx_parse.py extracting: core properties (title, author , created) and a linear list of blocks {kind: heading|paragraph|table, text, level?}. Use python-docx. Add unit tests for a tiny generated DOCX. python-docx documentation covers creating/updating .docx and exposes core_properties fields. Chunking and normalization: Unstructured policy Prompt template (Codex): “Implement semantic chunking with Unstructured” Add src/chunking/unstructured_chunk.py:
Use Unstructured partition output as elements.
Implement chunking strategy basic and by_title.
Output chunks[] where each chunk has {chunk_id, text, metadata:{doc_id,

page?, element_types[]}}. Add docs in references/chunking.md explaining policy. Unstructured documents describe partitioning into structural elements and chunking those elements for RAG, with strategies like basic and by_title. Embeddings + vector index: SentenceTransformers + FAISS/Qdrant Prompt template (Codex): “Build embeddings pipeline with caching” Create src/index/embed_index.py:
Use SentenceTransformers to generate embeddings for chunks.
Store embeddings in a local FAISS index for dev mode.
Also implement optional Qdrant REST upsert/search for prod mode.
Cache embeddings by sha256(text) to avoid recompute.

Provide CLI commands: embed, upsert, search. SentenceTransformers computes embeddings; FAISS supports similarity search; Qdrant provides REST API for managing vectors with payload metadata. Python snippet: SentenceTransformers + FAISS (minimal) fromsentence_transformersimportSentenceTransformer importfaiss importnumpyasnp model= SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2") # example texts= ["chunk one", "chunk two"] emb= model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True).astype("float32")

index= faiss.IndexFlatIP(emb.shape[1]) # cosine via normalized vectors + inner product index.add(emb) q = model.encode(["query"],normalize_embeddings=True).astype("float32") scores, ids= index.search(q, k=5) SentenceTransformers provides embedding APIs; FAISS supports similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. Qdrant REST pattern (pseudocode) // pseudocode POST/collections/{name}/points
"points":[ { "id":"chunk_001", "vector":[0.1, ...],"payload": { "doc_id":"doc1", "page":2, "tenant_id":"t1"} }
POST/collections/{name}/points/search
"vector":[0.2, ...], "limit": 5, "filter": { "must":[{"key":"tenant_id", "match": { "value":"t1"} }]}

Qdrant’s API reference positions it as a vector database with REST APIs and payloads; its docs describe vectors/points concepts used throughout APIs. JSON Schema validation: Ajv and python-jsonschema Prompt template (Codex): “Add schema validation gates + CI” Add schemas/slidespec.schema.json (draft 2020-12). Implement validation in both:
Python (python-jsonschema) for server-side.
JS (ajv) for front-end/Node tools.
Fail fast with clear error paths. Add CI step to validate sample payloads under examples/. Ajv supports JSON Schema drafts including 2020‑12; python‑jsonschema documents validate() usage.

Python snippet: schema validate fromjsonschemaimportvalidate defvalidate_slidespec(slidespec: dict, schema: dict) ->None: validate(instance=slidespec, schema=schema) Basic validation flow is documented in python‑jsonschema docs. SlideSpec assembly: brief → outline → SlideSpec with citations Prompt template (Codex): “Generate SlideSpec strictly from schema + retrieved evidence” You must output ONLY JSON that validates against schemas/slidespec.schema.json. Input: outline.json plus retrieved_chunks.json.
Rules:
Every factual numeric claim must include a citation {source_id, locator}.
Prefer short headlines.
Use these template_keys only: […]

Return slidespec.json. This is a recommended practice rather than a Codex-specific documented feature; tool calling and schema validation are grounded in OpenAI tool calling docs and JSON Schema tooling. Layout solving: rules → ResolvedLayout Prompt template (Codex): “Implement template-based layout resolver” Implement src/layout/resolve_layout.py:
Input: validated SlideSpec.
Output: ResolvedLayout with inches-based coordinates.
Use strict templates (title, 2-col, chart, table).
Add QA checks: overlap, out-of-bounds, min font size.
If fails, return diagnostics and a suggested “tighten text” edit.

Deterministic layout control is analogous to imperative, atomic batch updates in slide APIs; Google’s batchUpdate model illustrates structured slide operations and atomic validation. PPTX renderers: python-pptx and PptxGenJS Prompt template (Codex): “Render PPTX from ResolvedLayout” Create src/render/render_pptx.py using python-pptx.
Read ResolvedLayout JSON.
For each slide, add textboxes/images/tables.
Embed all images from local cache.
Emit deck.pptx and asset_manifest.json including sha256 hashes.

Add a regression test that opens the saved PPTX and validates slide count. python-pptx is documented as creating/updating .pptx files without requiring PowerPoint and is commonly used to generate PPTX from dynamic content, including JSON payloads. python-pptx minimal rendering snippet frompptximportPresentation frompptx.utilimportInches defrender(resolved_layout: dict, out_path: str): prs= Presentation() fors inresolved_layout["slides"]: slide= prs.slides.add_slide(prs.slide_layouts[6]) # blank forelins["elements"]: x, y, w, h = map(Inches, (el["x"],el["y"],el["w"],el["h"])) ifel["kind"] =="textbox": shape= slide.shapes.add_textbox(x, y, w, h) shape.text_frame.text= el["payload"]["text"] elifel["kind"] =="image": slide.shapes.add_picture(el["payload"]["path"],x, y, w, h) prs.save(out_path) python-pptx documentation describes presentation objects, slides, and creation/updating workflows.

PptxGenJS minimal rendering snippet importPptxGenJSfrom"pptxgenjs"; exportasyncfunctionrender(layout, outPath) { constpptx= newPptxGenJS(); pptx.layout= "LAYOUT_WIDE"; for(consts oflayout.slides) { constslide= pptx.addSlide(); for(constelofs.elements) { if(el.kind==="textbox") { slide.addText(el.payload.text, { x: el.x, y: el.y, w: el.w, h: el.h });
if(el.kind==="image") { slide.addImage({path: el.payload.path, x: el.x, y: el.y, w: el.w, h: el.h });
awaitpptx.writeFile({fileName: outPath});

PptxGenJS documents addSlide() usage and positions itself as generating OOXML-compatible PPTX; it also documents slide masters for branding. OOXML correctness and debugging workflow When renderers break, Codex can implement a “zip-open and inspect the PPTX parts” workflow. Microsoft’s Open XML SDK documentation describes .pptx as a package where you can rename to .zip and inspect parts (presentation.xml, slides, relationships). ECMA‑376 defines the overall packaging and markup requirements. Best practices for chaining Codex skills into reliable slide pipelines Codex documentation emphasizes habits that improve results—prompting, planning, validation, skills, MCP , and careful workflows. In slide pipelines, these translate into concrete engineering patterns. Recommended pipeline gates Gate on parsing quality Keep a QA report per document: pages extracted, OCR used, tables found, and “empty page” rates. This is motivated by the known limitations of PDF parsing vs scanned PDFs (OCR requirement).

Gate on chunking and retrieval Adopt a consistent chunking strategy that respects embedding model limits and improves retrieval precision; Unstructured’s chunking docs explicitly frame chunking as a post-partition step to support RAG and model limits. Gate on schema validation (hard fail) Validate SlideSpec and ResolvedLayout via JSON Schema—before layout solving, before rendering, and after any “design revise” step. Ajv and python‑jsonschema provide standard tooling for this. Gate on OOXML/PPTX integrity Add a “round-trip check” that opens or inspects the PPTX package and verifies slide count, embedded media presence, and required parts. Microsoft’s Open XML SDK docs provide a reference mental model for required parts. Operational engineering practices Retries, idempotency, and caching Codex should implement chunk IDs and asset IDs that are deterministic (e.g., SHA‑256), enabling

idempotent upserts into FAISS/Qdrant and avoiding duplicate slides/assets. This aligns well with vector DB payload semantics and points-based APIs. Dependency pinning and reproducible environments Because PPTX and PDF parsing behavior can be version-sensitive, Codex should pin versions in requirements.txt/lockfiles and include CI smoke tests. Codex itself documents configuration files and reproducible setups (config references). Skills packaging Use Codex skills as repeatable modules: one skill per major pipeline stage (parse, index, slidespec, render). Codex skills documentation and OpenAI’s skills/Agents SDK blog emphasize progressive disclosure and organizing skills in .agents/skills. Codex-specific failure modes and mitigations This section focuses on failure modes that are common with coding agents and are called out by Codex best-practices style guidance (validation, testing, cautious automation). Hallucinated or non-running code Failure mode: Codex outputs code that looks plausible but fails at runtime, especially around tricky file formats (OOXML) and PDF edge cases.

Mitigations: * Require Codex to run tests locally (in the workspace) after implementing changes; Codex CLI is designed to run commands in the directory. Add golden‑file tests: known input documents → expected parsed JSON; known SlideSpec → expected slide count and key text presence. Use strict schema validation to catch “almost right” JSON. Unsafe file handling and path traversal Failure mode: writing files outside intended directories, reading secrets, or embedding external URLs unsafely into PPTX rendering paths. Mitigations: * Restrict allowed directories; enforce safe path joins; forbid .. traversal; validate MIME types.
Use Codex configuration and repo-scoped operation patterns; Codex is documented as operating within

the selected directory and configurable via config files. Long-running and resource-heavy jobs Failure mode: OCR or embedding batches take too long, time out, or exhaust memory (FAISS indexes, image rendering). Mitigations: * Implement chunk batching, incremental checkpointing, and resumable jobs (job state persisted). Prefer asynchronous job models for export/render (status polling), modeled after common API patterns (export jobs as a concept). Use model selection and reasoning effort settings appropriately; OpenAI model docs describe options and token limits that affect throughput and cost.

Dependency drift and format brittleness Failure mode: minor library updates change parsing or rendering behavior; OOXML correctness issues produce corrupted PPTX. Mitigations: * Pin versions and add regression tests.
Use standards references and known-good template PPTX files as bases; Open XML SDK documentation

describes the structure of minimal presentations and required parts. Security, privacy, and IP considerations for using Codex in these workflows Data use and retention: OpenAI vs your environment OpenAI’s enterprise privacy commitments state that business data is not used to train models by default for qualifying business products and the API platform, and customers own inputs/outputs where allowed by law. OpenAI also documents API data controls: abuse monitoring logs may include prompts/responses and are retained for up to 30 days by default (subject to legal requirements). For individual services (including Codex), OpenAI’s help/policy materials describe that content may be used to train models depending on your settings and opt-out controls; you should align plan selection and data controls with your compliance needs. Assumption: If you run Codex CLI locally, your sensitive documents may still be processed by remote models depending on how you authenticate and configure Codex. Treat this as a governance decision: choose enterprise/API configurations with appropriate retention/training settings.

Sandboxing, secrets, and least privilege Codex is described as an agent that can operate on a repo and run commands; OpenAI’s “Introducing Codex” announcement describes a cloud-based engineering agent where tasks run in a sandbox environment preloaded with your repository. Regardless of local vs cloud, you should enforce least privilege for file access and secrets. Practical controls Codex can implement (recommended): Never print secrets in logs; use environment variables injected at runtime; scrub stack traces. Separate “parse” workers from “render” workers; deny network access in render stage if possible. Store provenance and access controls with each chunk and asset (tenant_id, allowed audiences). IP and licensing for generated assets and third-party inputs Even if Codex writes the pipeline, IP risk comes from: The source documents you ingest (copyright, confidentiality).

Image assets you embed (stock licenses, AI-generation terms). OCR outputs that reproduce copyrighted content. Codex can help by generating an asset manifest with license/attribution fields and by hashing embedded assets. OOXML/PPTX parts are embedded inside the package; Microsoft’s docs describe how images/media live as parts referenced in slide XML. Skill-to-prompt-to-library mapping table The table below maps each major “Codex skill” to a concrete prompt pattern, IO expectations, and libraries. It is intended as a build checklist for .agents/skills organization. Skills as an authoring/distribution format are documented in Codex docs (SKILL.md + metadata). Codex skills/ progressive disclosure Integration checklist for a Codex-assisted SlideSpec agent This checklist is designed to be short, practical, and aligned with Codex’s “skills + validation” workflow philosophy. Define schemas/ for SlideSpec, ResolvedLayout, AssetManifest, ExportJob; add Ajv + python- jsonschema validators as mandatory gates.

Implement ingestion parsers with OCR fallback and explicit QA reporting (pages extracted, OCR pages, tables). Choose chunking policy (Unstructured basic / by_title or your own), and standardize chunk IDs and metadata. Build embeddings + vector store with deterministic IDs and caching; enforce tenant filters in retrieval. Implement brief→outline→SlideSpec prompt chain that requires citations using retrieved chunks; fail when evidence missing. Implement a layout resolver with overlap/out-of-bounds checks; produce diagnostics for “tighten text” revisions. Render PPTX via python‑pptx or PptxGenJS; produce an asset manifest and a PPTX integrity check (unzip/inspect). Package each step as a Codex skill (.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md + scripts + references) using progressive disclosure to keep context slim. Confirm data governance: choose OpenAI product tier and data controls appropriate for your sensitivity; document retention/training settings and secret-handling policies.

chunks.text: How AI Presentation Systems Ingest Data and

Generate Visually Polished PPTX Decks Executive summary AI “PowerPoint generators” that produce visually appealing decks are usually not just a single LLM that “writes slides.” In practice, high-quality systems converge on a hybrid architecture: (a) structured data ingestion and normalization, (b) retrieval/grounding (often via RAG-style retrieval), (c) multi-step content planning and slide outlining, (d) template- and rule-driven layout with explicit constraints, (e) asset selection/generation with style control, and (f) deterministic export to PPTX / OOXML. This separation is visible in several commercial offerings—e.g., outline-first then generate slides flows and template- preserving generation endpoints. Among representative systems, three practical “design quality” strategies dominate: Template-first, variable substitution: Keep a fixed (often brand-approved) layout and swap content into placeholders. This reduces layout failures and is explicitly exposed by some APIs (e.g., “generate from template”). Rule-based auto-layout (“smart slides”): Use curated slide types and layout rules that auto-resize/ align as content changes, trading flexibility for polish and predictability.

Free-form layout generation with constraint checks: Let the model propose structure, then enforce geometric/readability constraints (no overlap, alignment, margins, contrast). Modern research benchmarks for slide generation explicitly evaluate these properties. For “data ingestion,” production systems typically provide multiple entry points: prompt + pasted text, document upload (Word/PDF), linked cloud files, and structured data connectors (spreadsheets/APIs). Official docs from major ecosystems explicitly emphasize these modalities (e.g., Drive/Graph grounding, and data connectors that keep external data linked and refreshable). A reproducible, stack-agnostic way to match commercial quality is to introduce an internal intermediate representation—a strict SlideSpec JSON—and treat the LLM as one component that outputs constrained structures, which then flow through deterministic layout + PPTX rendering (OOXML). The PPTX format is standardized under ECMA-376 / ISO/IEC 29500, and practical libraries generate “standards-compliant OOXML” without requiring an installed PowerPoint app.

Landscape of AI presentation tools Representative tools and where “visual polish” comes from The table below focuses on tools that either (a) have documented AI presentation creation flows, or (b) expose APIs that programmatically generate/export PPTX, or (c) are open-source engines you can self-host.

control and inspectability; more engineering burden (hosting, model choice, template design); “visual quality” depends heavily on your templates and style rules A practical categorization of “architectures” in the wild Even when internal implementations are proprietary, official docs expose enough surface area to infer three dominant architectures: Suite-integrated copilot: The AI drafts and edits inside the presentation editor , grounded in tenant file systems (Graph/Drive) and existing templates. Prompt-to-deck SaaS: A service accepts text (and sometimes docs) and returns a deck with a theme, often via asynchronous generation and export URLs. Template engine + AI fill: The platform’s differentiator is a structured layout vocabulary (template types, masters, constraints) and auto-layout rules; the AI mainly chooses templates and populates content.

End-to-end pipeline reference architecture This section defines a stack-agnostic pipeline that reproduces the capabilities demonstrated in official tool documentation and relevant research: outline-first authoring, template-driven layouts, retrieval-grounded content, and deterministic PPTX export. Core components Assumptions (explicit): A “text LLM” exists that can produce JSON under constraints (any vendor/model). An “embedding model” exists (open-source or hosted). A vector store exists (in-memory or external). A PPTX renderer exists (OOXML-based; e.g., JS or Python OSS libraries). Key services: Ingestion service: accepts documents, URLs, and structured records; outputs normalized “content objects” with metadata, chunks, and permissions. Indexing service: computes embeddings and writes to a vector index; maintains citation pointers to sources. Planning/orchestration service: multi-stage prompt chain: brief → outline → slide-by-slide intents → SlideSpec JSON. Layout service: selects slide templates/masters and resolves geometry constraints (margins, alignment, no overlap). Asset service: selects/generates images/icons/charts with cohesive style; validates licenses.

Renderer/exporter: converts resolved layout into OOXML PPTX; validates round-trip in PowerPoint/ Slides; produces artifacts and manifests. Mermaid flowchart of the reference pipeline flowchart TD A[Inputs: prompt, docs, URLs, tables, DB rows] --> B[Ingestion & parsing] B --> C[Normalization: elements, tables, figures, metadata] C --> D[Chunking + dedup + PII redaction] D --> E[Embeddings / vectorization] E --> F[Vector index + metadata store] A --> G[User constraints: audience, tone, time, brand rules] G --> H[Content planning LLM: brief -> outline] H --> I[Slide planning LLM: per-slide intents + key messages] F --> J[RAG retrieval: top-k chunks per slide] I --> K[SlideSpec generation LLM: strict JSON schema]
J --> K
K --> L[Template selection + layout constraints solver] L --> M[Asset selection / generation & styling] M --> N[PPTX rendering (OOXML)] N --> O[QA: overflow, overlap, contrast, citations]

O --> P[Export: .pptx + manifest + logs] O -->|fail| I This structure matches patterns visible in documented products: outline-first drafting with clarifying questions, template-based generation endpoints, and asynchronous export workflows that return share URLs and downloadable PPTX/PDF links. Data ingestion, preprocessing, vectorization, and retrieval Ingestion modalities and “data plumbing” Across ecosystems, ingestion tends to fall into four data shapes: Free-form text: plain prompt or pasted outline. (Common to most systems.) Structured authoring sources: Word/Docs with headings, styles, and embedded images—explicitly recommended as inputs to improve structure detection and slide breakdown. Cloud file grounding: referencing Drive or tenant content when generating new slides. Linked structured data: connectors that import external data and keep it refreshable (e.g., data connectors). A notable “agentic” integration pattern is MCP (Model Context Protocol), positioned as a standard to connect AI tools to external systems with structured tools and authorization.

This appears both in ecosystem-level announcements and in platform implementations (e.g., MCP server references for design workflows). Parsing and normalization For real-world enterprise decks, document parsing is often the hardest part. A common open-source approach is structure-first parsing into typed elements (titles, narrative text, list items, tables), then chunking those elements into retrieval-ready pieces (as opposed to naive “split by characters”). This is explicitly how the unstructured library describes partitioning and chunking. Vectorization and retrieval (RAG) For grounding, modern systems often implement retrieval-augmented generation (RAG): retrieve top-k chunks from a dense vector index and condition generation on them. The canonical formulation combines parametric generation with non-parametric memory accessed via dense retrieval. Dense vectorization is commonly implemented with sentence embedding models such as SBERT-style bi- encoders, enabling cosine-similarity search at scale. Data ingestion JSON schema and example payload Below is a concrete JSON Schema for an ingestion API that produces normalized content suitable for slide generation and citations.
(This is a reference design; implementations typically maintain additional internal fields for tenancy and security.)

"required": ["columns", "rows"], "additionalProperties": false

"required": ["asset_id", "content_type"], "additionalProperties": false

"options": { "type": "object", "properties": { "chunking": { "type": "string", "enum": ["none", "by_element", "semantic"],"default": "by_element"}, "redact_pii": { "type": "boolean", "default": true}, "max_chunk_chars": { "type": "integer", "default": 1200}

"source": { "type": "upload", "id": "upl_9f2c2b", "retrieved_at": "2026-04-04T14:10:00Z", "permissions": { "tenant_id": "t_acme", "allowed_principals": ["user:jsmith", "group:finance"] }
"document": { "title": "Q1 Business Review", "mime_type": "application/pdf", "language": "en", "elements": [ { "element_id": "e1", "type": "heading", "text": "Revenue Overview", "page": 2 },
"element_id": "e2", "type": "table", "page": 2, "table": { "columns": ["Region", "Q1 Revenue", "QoQ"], "rows": [["NA", 12.4, "+6%"],["EMEA", 9.8, "+2%"],["APAC", 7.1,
"element_id": "e3", "type": "figure", "page": 3, "asset_ref": { "asset_id": "asset_img_77", "content_type": "image/png", "sha256": "..."}
"options": { "chunking": "by_element", "redact_pii": true, "max_chunk_chars":
This structure aligns with “element-first” parsing and chunking approaches described in unstructured, and supports downstream RAG with provenance metadata. 34

Parsing + embeddings + index pseudocode # Pseudocode (stack-agnostic) defingest_and_index(file_bytes, mime_type, doc_id, embed_model, vector_index, metadata_store): # 1) Parse into typed elements (titles, lists, tables, figures) elements= parse_to_elements(file_bytes, mime_type) # e.g., structure- first parsing # 2) Normalize + chunk chunks= [] forelinelements: forchunkinchunk_element(el, max_chars=1200): chunks.append({ "chunk_id": f"{doc_id}:{el['element_id']}:{chunk['i']}", "text": chunk["text"], "meta": { "doc_id": doc_id, "element_id": el["element_id"], "page": el.get("page"), "type": el["type"]
# 3) Embed

vectors= embed_model.encode([c["text"] forc inchunks]) # 4) Write to index + metadata store forc, v inzip(chunks, vectors): vector_index.upsert(id=c["chunk_id"],vector=v, metadata=c["meta"]) metadata_store.put_chunk(c["chunk_id"],c) return{"doc_id": doc_id, "n_elements": len(elements),"n_chunks": len(chunks)} The key design choice is to preserve structure metadata (page, element type) to enable better retrieval and citation linking, which is a core motivation in element-based partitioning/chunking approaches. 35

Slide specification, layout generation, and PPTX export Why a SlideSpec intermediate representation matters Commercial systems that reliably produce “good-looking” slides typically constrain generation into an internal schema rather than letting an LLM emit arbitrary layout commands. Evidence for this appears indirectly: Template-based endpoints explicitly preserve layout while swapping content (“variable substitution”). Smart-slide systems expose “multiple layout options” and auto-alignment behavior , strongly implying a fixed vocabulary of layout templates with rule-based transforms. Research on visual-textual presentation layout explicitly models templates, constraints, and optimization objectives (e.g., font constraints, color harmonization, and typography optimization), which mirrors how production systems enforce readability and aesthetics. A SlideSpec acts as the contract between content generation and rendering, enabling deterministic PPTX outputs and automatic QA (overflow, overlap, inadequate contrast).

PPTX export realities PPTX is part of the standardized Office Open XML formats (ECMA-376 / ISO/IEC 29500), and is implemented as an OPC/ZIP package containing XML parts and relationships. Practical exporters commonly generate OOXML directly: python-pptx creates/reads/updates .pptx without requiring PowerPoint installed. PptxGenJS outputs standards-compliant OOXML and supports master slides for branding. SlideSpec JSON schema and example payload Below is a concrete JSON Schema for a SlideSpec that supports both template-driven and free-form layouts.

"$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "$id": "https://example.com/schemas/slide-spec.schema.json", "title": "PresentationSpec", "type": "object", "required": ["title", "slides", "theme"], "properties": { "title": { "type": "string"}, "audience": { "type": "string"}, "language": { "type": "string", "default": "en-US"}, "theme": { "type": "object", "required": ["name", "tokens"],
"properties": { "name": { "type": "string"}, "tokens": { "$ref": "https://example.com/schemas/style- tokens.schema.json"}

"slides": { "type": "array", "minItems": 1, "items": { "type": "object", "required": ["slide_id", "purpose", "layout_intent", "blocks"], "properties": { "slide_id": { "type": "string"}, "purpose": { "type": "string", "enum": ["title", "agenda", "section", "content", "summary", "appendix"] }, "layout_intent": { "type": "object", "required": ["template_key"], "properties": { "template_key": { "type": "string", "description": "Logical template name, e.g. 'title.hero', '2col.text_image', 'kpi.3up'"
"strict_template": { "type": "boolean", "default": true}

"headline": { "type": "string"}, "speaker_notes": { "type": "string"}, "blocks": { "type": "array", "minItems": 1, "items": { "type": "object", "required": ["block_id", "kind"], "properties": { "block_id": { "type": "string"}, "kind": { "type": "string", "enum": ["text", "bullets", "image", "table", "chart", "icon_row", "quote", "callout"] }, "content": {}, "source_citations": { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object", "required": ["source_id", "locator"],
"properties": { "source_id": { "type": "string"}, "locator": { "type": "string", "description": "e.g. doc_id:page or url#section"}, "quote": { "type": "string"}

"style_overrides": { "type": "object", "properties": { "emphasis": { "type": "string", "enum": ["none", "low", "medium", "high"] }, "color_role": { "type": "string", "enum": ["primary", "secondary", "accent", "muted"] }

"title": "Q1 Business Review", "audience": "Executive leadership", "language": "en-US", "theme": { "name": "Acme Corporate 2026", "tokens": { "fonts": { "heading": "Aptos Display", "body": "Aptos"}, "colors": { "bg": "#FFFFFF", "text": "#1A1A1A", "accent": "#2F6BFF"}, "spacing": { "margin_in": 0.6, "gutter_in": 0.3}

This mirrors the documented reality that high-quality systems organize work around outlines, templates, and layout constraints rather than “raw drawing commands.” Layout JSON schema and example payload The layout resolver translates SlideSpec into precise coordinates. A separate schema keeps the renderer deterministic.

Style token JSON schema and example payload This schema is inspired by “design tokens” approaches: portable style primitives that can be mapped to PPTX font/color APIs and to template systems.

img_path= resolve_asset_path(el["data_ref"]) slide.shapes.add_picture(img_path, x, y, w, h) # tables/charts often require additional plumbing: # either render as an image, or construct table shapes. returnprs Pattern B: JavaScript exporter (OOXML-based libraries) // Pseudocode for layout -> PPTX using a JS OOXML generator constpptx= newPptxGenJS(); pptx.layout= "LAYOUT_WIDE"; // 16:9 for(constslideSpecoflayout.slides) { constslide= pptx.addSlide(); for(constelofslideSpec.elements) { if(el.kind==="textbox") { slide.addText(resolveText(el.data_ref),{ x: el.x, y: el.y, w: el.w, h: el.h, fontFace: tokens.fonts.body, color: tokens.colors.text.replace("#", "")
if(el.kind==="image") { slide.addImage({path: resolveAsset(el.data_ref),x: el.x, y: el.y, w: el.w, h: el.h});

awaitpptx.writeFile({fileName: "deck.pptx"}); These patterns are aligned with the documented capabilities of OOXML/PPTX generation libraries: creating slides, adding text/images, and using slide masters for branding. Prompt templates and multi-step prompt chains Why multi-step prompting is the default in polished systems Multiple official products describe staged workflows: Outline-first with refinement before generating full slides.

Clarifying questions about audience, style, and image preferences before slide generation. This suggests it’s operationally valuable to split “reasoning” across steps with explicit contracts (schemas), and to run quality checks between steps. Prompt chain blueprint Below is a reproducible chain that separates (1) content fidelity, (2) story/structure, and (3) design/layout decisions. Step zero: system policy for deck generation SYSTEM: You are a presentation writer + slide designer. Goals: accurate, audience-appropriate, visually scannable slides.
Constraints:
Prefer short headlines (<= 8 words).
Each slide: single point; 3-5 bullets max; avoid dense paragraphs.
Use citations: every non-trivial factual claim must cite a source_id +
locator.
Output must conform to the provided JSON Schema exactly.
If information is missing, ask for it via "questions_for_user" rather than
guessing. Step one: content brief + constraints collection USER: Create a deck brief.
Inputs:
Audience: {audience}
Goal: {goal}
Time: {talk_length_minutes}
Brand style: {style_tokens_summary}
Source corpus: {list_of_source_ids}
Return JSON:
"one_sentence_thesis": "...", "key_takeaways": ["...", "...", "..."], "slide_count_target": 10, "tone": "...", "open_questions": ["..."]
This mirrors the “clarifying questions then outline” behavior described for in-editor copilots. Step two: outline (slide-by-slide plan) USER: Given the brief JSON, propose a slide outline.
Rules:
Slide types: title, agenda, section, content, summary, appendix
For each slide include: purpose, headline, 1-sentence message, required
evidence needs. Return JSON:
"outline": [
"slide_id": "s1", "purpose": "title", "headline": "...", "message": "...", "evidence_queries": ["..."]

This “draft in stages” pattern is explicitly described in AI presentation workflows. Step three: retrieval planning (RAG query generation per slide) USER: For each outline item, generate up to 5 retrieval queries. Return JSON:
"retrieval_plan": [
"slide_id": "s2", "queries": ["...", "..."], "filters": { "doc_ids": ["doc_q1_pdf"], "min_date": null }
This step makes RAG controllable and auditable, aligning with the motivation of retrieval-grounded generation (provenance and updateability).
Step four: SlideSpec generation under strict schema USER: Generate PresentationSpec JSON adhering exactly to schema X. You will be given:
outline
retrieved_chunks_per_slide (with chunk_id, text, source_id, locator)
Every factual claim must link to source_citations.
Prefer strict_template=true unless content requires custom geometry.
Avoid more than 40 words of body text per slide unless slide purpose=appendix.
Step five: design revision prompts (layout and style iteration) A “designer” prompt that does not change facts, only improves clarity and aesthetics: USER: Revise SlideSpec focusing on design:
Reduce text density.
Replace generic visuals with specific visuals (icon, photo, chart) where it
helps.
Ensure consistent image style per style tokens.
Keep citations unchanged (may add, not remove).

Return updated SlideSpec JSON only. This parallels tools that expose “layout variants” and “multiple layout options” after generation. Evaluation metrics and failure modes Quality gates used in research and in production-like checks Recent slide-generation benchmarks explicitly evaluate layout and color properties such as: Elements stay within the slide boundary No overlap Proper alignment High-contrast colors and avoidance of overly glaring palettes Production systems can implement these as deterministic validators on the ResolvedDeckLayout. A practical evaluation rubric (automatable + human-graded): Content fidelity * Citation coverage: % of factual statements with citations (target: ~100% for externally sourced facts)

Source alignment: entailment-style checks between slide claims and retrieved chunks (sampled human review) Retrieval quality: recall@k on a labeled set of “must-include” passages (RAG practice; grounded in dense retrieval literature) Design quality * Overflow count: number of text boxes where computed line breaks exceed height Overlap ratio: total area overlap / slide area Alignment score: variance from grid lines (or template anchors)
Contrast heuristics: WCAG-like contrast estimate for foreground/background pairs (especially for text on
images) Export integrity * Round-trip diffs: export → re-open in PowerPoint/Slides → check missing fonts, broken images, shifted elements
External dependency checks: image URLs used in generation remain accessible (not short-lived signed

URLs) Common failure modes and how to mitigate them Text overflow / “wall of text” slides Root causes: LLM verbosity; poor template selection; missing density limits. Mitigations: enforce per-slide max words, and use outline-first editing; auto-style “tighten text” pass; prefer “appendix” slides for detail. Outline-first workflows are explicitly recommended/implemented in commercial systems. Broken images / missing assets Root causes: referenced image URLs expire or require auth headers; export URLs expire. Gamma’s API docs explicitly warn that Gamma stores references to image URLs and highlights risks of short-lived signed URLs; Canva export URLs expire after 24 hours. Mitigations: copy/rehydrate images into your own object store; validate URL accessibility before generation; store a local cached version for PPTX embedding. Design inconsistency across slides Root causes: unconstrained image styles, ad-hoc color choices, no template system. Mitigations: enforce theme tokens; pick image source policy and consistent image style before generation (documented as product features).

PPTX export mismatch Root causes: platform-specific features (animations/audio) don’t map 1:1 to PPTX; different layout engines. Beautiful.ai explicitly warns that certain elements (e.g., animations/audio) may not transfer when exporting to PowerPoint. Mitigations: design with “export-safe” primitives (text, images, shapes, tables); avoid complex animations unless your renderer supports them; run automated PPTX re-open tests.

Privacy, IP , and licensing considerations Training and data use policies vary materially by vendor Enterprise buyers should not assume uniform “no training on your content” defaults. Microsoft’s commercial Copilot documentation emphasizes that prompts/responses and Graph- accessed data are not used to train foundation models for Microsoft 365 Copilot, and that customer data is kept within Microsoft’s service boundary. Gamma’s privacy policy states it may use personal information (including user-generated content) for research and development, including to train its AI models, and may create aggregated/de- identified data. Beautiful.ai’s security page states that AI subprocessors are barred from using customer data for training and describes controls around generative AI integrations. Canva’s terms describe privacy settings that allow users to control whether usage data and/or user content can improve AI-powered features; Canva also provides AI product terms noting that outputs may not be unique.

These differences imply a concrete architectural recommendation: treat “deck generation” systems as data processors, and implement tenant-level controls: logging, retention, redaction, and explicit opt-in/opt-out paths per data class. IP risks in slide generation workflows Key risk surfaces: Source document rights: If you ingest third-party PDFs, articles, or proprietary reports, you must have rights to reproduce or transform them into slides. Generated images: If you use text-to-image models, licensing terms may impose downstream obligations and usage restrictions. The CreativeML Open RAIL-M license (used for Stable Diffusion code/models in common distributions) includes explicit licensing terms and restrictions. Non-uniqueness of outputs: Some AI product terms explicitly state that outputs may not be unique and other users may receive similar outputs—important for brand differentiation and IP expectations. Training data disputes and evolving guidance: Government and policy analyses emphasize that training uses copyrighted works and legal questions remain active and jurisdiction-dependent.

Practical mitigations: Maintain a content manifest: every slide element links to a source record (doc/page/url + timestamp). Offer a “no web / no model training” mode (or vendor selection aligned to enterprise commitments). Prefer licensed stock libraries or customer-provided assets for external-facing decks; if using generative images, store provenance metadata where available (some products reference C2PA-style provenance).

Compliance and export security Two frequently overlooked but operationally critical details from official API docs: Short-lived export URLs: Canva export job results return download URLs that expire after 24 hours. External image references: Gamma warns that expiring image URLs can lead to broken images; recommends long-lived URLs and avoiding authenticated/internal URLs. For regulated environments, this implies the exporter should support a hardened mode: Download and embed all external assets into the PPTX at render time Produce an immutable archive: PPTX + asset hashes + licensing notes + generation logs Run DLP/PII scans on extracted text before sending to any external model endpoint (if any) Create a new presentation with Copilot in PowerPoint -
