Oracle HCM Cloud 26B Release Notes Global Human Resources
Release Date: June 2026 Document Type: Feature Summary and Implementation Guidance
Overview

Oracle HCM Cloud 26B delivers targeted enhancements to Global Human Resources with a focus on workforce structure flexibility, employment model accuracy, and compliance-driven reporting. This release addresses long-standing customer requests around position management, legislative data group assignments, and person record governance. Customers on Revision 1 or later of the 26A patch are eligible to adopt all features described below.
New Features
Workforce Structures

Position Synchronization with Job Profile Positions can now inherit job profile attributes automatically when the associated job is updated. Previously, customers managing large position inventories had to manually realign position titles, grades, and competency requirements after job catalog changes. The new synchronization rule can be configured at the business unit level and supports selective inheritance, meaning administrators can choose which attributes flow down from job to position without overwriting position-specific overrides.

Default Hire Date Offset by Location A new location-level configuration option allows HR administrators to define a standard hire date offset for positions in specific countries or legal employers. This supports organizations where regulatory requirements mandate a waiting period between offer acceptance and the official employment start date. The offset applies automatically during the hire transaction and generates a pending worker record for the intervening period.

Grade Ladder Enforcement at Position Level Grade ladders can now be enforced directly on positions rather than solely at the job level. This provides finer control in organizations that allow the same job to span multiple grade ladders depending on the business unit or department. Validation occurs at the point of assignment creation and produces an actionable error message when a proposed grade falls outside the position-assigned ladder.
Person Management

Preferred Name Display in Workforce Directory Employees can now designate a preferred first name that displays throughout the HCM interface, including Workforce Directory, My Team, and manager self-service pages. Legal name continues to be used in payroll, benefits, and compliance reporting. Administrators can restrict preferred name use by legal employer if local regulations require legal name display in employee-facing systems.

National Identifier Masking by Role National identifier values such as national ID numbers and social security numbers can now be masked at the role level rather than requiring a custom security profile for each use case. The masking configuration is managed in the Security Console and supports partial masking, full masking, or clear text display based on the user's assigned data role. Audit logging captures each unmasked view event.

Corrective Action on Historical Work Relationships A new corrective action flow allows HR specialists to amend historical assignment records without triggering a full rehire or termination sequence. The correction is date-effective and supports changes to employment type, legal employer, and business unit for closed work relationships. All corrections are recorded in the worker audit trail and visible in the Employment History report.
Global Compliance and Reporting

Legislative Data Group Assignment Validation 26B introduces a pre-submission validation step in the legislative data group assignment flow. When an HR administrator assigns a worker to a new LDG, the system now checks for active payroll relationships, outstanding expense reports, and pending benefit elections tied to the current LDG before completing the transfer. A summary of blocking and warning conditions is presented before the administrator confirms the action.

Work Permit and Visa Tracking Enhancements The Document Records feature has been extended with two new document categories specific to work authorization: Permit to Work and Right to Work Verification. Each category includes configurable expiration alerting, document owner assignment, and a review workflow that notifies the HR specialist thirty, sixty, and ninety days before expiration. The tracking records are visible in the worker's Employment Info page and in the Workforce Compliance Dashboard.

Country-Specific Absence Accrual Reporting New country-level absence accrual reports are available for Australia, Germany, Netherlands, and the United Kingdom. Each report reflects the legislative accrual model for that country and can be run as a point-in-time snapshot or as a period summary. The reports are accessible from the Absence Administration work area and support export to CSV and PDF.
Known Issues and Workarounds

Position Synchronization with Job Profile does not propagate changes to positions that have an active open requisition. Customers using Recruiting must manually update position attributes after closing or canceling the linked requisition. Oracle is tracking this as a known issue with a targeted fix in 26C.
Preferred Name Display is not yet available in the HCM Mobile application. Mobile will display legal first name until the 26C mobile release, which is targeted for September 2026.

Grade Ladder Enforcement at Position Level does not apply retroactively to existing assignments. Customers who wish to validate historical assignments against the new position-level grade ladder must run the Validate Assignment Grade Ladder concurrent process after enabling the feature.
Upgrade Considerations

Customers upgrading from 25B or earlier should review the Position Synchronization configuration before enabling automatic inheritance. Organizations with highly customized position structures or extensive use of position-specific grade overrides should conduct a test run in a non-production environment before activating synchronization in production.

The National Identifier Masking by Role feature replaces the legacy masking configuration available under Manage Person Security Profiles. Customers using the legacy configuration should migrate their masking rules to the Security Console before the 27A release, when legacy masking support will be removed.
Support and Resources

Oracle Support Note 3198447.1 contains the full list of bug fixes included in 26B. The HCM Cloud Customer Connect community hosts a 26B readiness webinar series beginning in May 2026. Implementation guidance for the Work Permit and Visa Tracking Enhancements is available in the Oracle Help Center under Global Human Resources, Document Records.
Oracle HCM Cloud Integration Approach Options Analysis: Connecting HR to Downstream Systems
Executive Summary

This document presents three architectural options for integrating Oracle HCM Cloud with downstream systems including payroll, benefits administration, identity management, and enterprise resource planning. Each option is evaluated on implementation complexity, total cost of ownership, data governance, and long-term maintainability. The recommendation section identifies a preferred approach based on the organization's current state architecture and strategic direction.

The three options under consideration are a Native Oracle Integration approach using Oracle Integration Cloud, a Middleware-Broker approach using the organization's existing MuleSoft platform, and a File-Based Extract approach using Oracle BI Publisher and scheduled SFTP transfers.
Option 1: Oracle Integration Cloud (OIC) Native Integration

Description Oracle Integration Cloud is Oracle's managed integration platform as a service. It provides pre-built adapters for Oracle HCM Cloud and a library of accelerator templates for common downstream connections including Oracle ERP Cloud, Workday, SAP, and ADP. OIC is provisioned within the Oracle Cloud tenancy and shares the same identity and access management infrastructure as HCM Cloud.

How It Works HR events in Oracle HCM Cloud, such as a new hire, termination, or compensation change, trigger HCM Atom Feeds or Business Events. OIC subscribes to these events, applies data transformation logic using visual mapping tools, and routes the transformed payload to the target system. Error handling, retry logic, and monitoring dashboards are built into the OIC platform.

Pros Pre-built HCM adapters reduce custom development time significantly. Oracle certifies adapter compatibility with each HCM quarterly release, reducing the maintenance burden during upgrades. The visual integration designer lowers the skill barrier for integration maintenance and allows HR IT staff to manage simple mappings without developer involvement. End-to-end monitoring is available in a single console. Connectivity to other Oracle Cloud services such as Oracle ERP and Oracle Planning is straightforward.

Cons OIC licensing adds ongoing subscription cost. Organizations already invested in a non-Oracle integration platform may face redundancy. OIC requires Oracle Cloud Infrastructure network connectivity, which adds complexity for hybrid environments where target systems are on-premises. Complex transformations involving multiple source systems can become difficult to manage visually at scale.
Estimated Implementation Effort Medium. Pre-built accelerators reduce time for standard connections. Custom connections to non-Oracle targets require additional development.
Total Cost of Ownership Moderate ongoing cost due to OIC subscription. Lower long-term maintenance cost compared to custom middleware due to Oracle-managed upgrades.
Option 2: MuleSoft Anypoint Platform (Existing Middleware Broker)

Description The organization currently operates a MuleSoft Anypoint Platform instance that manages integrations between enterprise applications. This option extends the existing MuleSoft environment to manage Oracle HCM Cloud integrations, using MuleSoft's HCM Connector and custom API specifications.

How It Works MuleSoft polls Oracle HCM Cloud using the HCM Extracts or REST APIs on a scheduled or event-driven basis. Data is transformed using DataWeave, MuleSoft's transformation language, and routed to downstream systems through existing MuleSoft channels. The integration governance, error handling, and alerting infrastructure already in place for other enterprise integrations is reused.

Pros Leverages existing platform investment and team expertise. A single integration governance layer covers HCM and all other enterprise integrations, simplifying monitoring and incident response. DataWeave is a powerful transformation language that handles complex mapping scenarios more elegantly than visual tools. The MuleSoft API-led connectivity model makes individual integrations reusable across multiple consumers.

Cons MuleSoft HCM Connector requires maintenance alignment with Oracle HCM Cloud API versioning. Each Oracle HCM quarterly release must be validated against the connector. DataWeave expertise is less common in HR IT teams, creating a dependency on centralized integration developers. MuleSoft licensing is substantial and is typically owned by Enterprise Architecture, creating a shared services dependency for HR integration changes.

Estimated Implementation Effort Low to medium for integrations that follow existing MuleSoft patterns. Higher for integrations requiring new connector development or complex transformation logic outside established patterns.
Total Cost of Ownership Lower incremental cost if MuleSoft licensing is already allocated. Higher risk of hidden cost from developer dependency and quarterly release validation cycles.
Option 3: File-Based Extract via Oracle BI Publisher and SFTP

Description Oracle HCM Cloud supports scheduled data extracts through Oracle BI Publisher and Oracle HCM Extracts. This option uses scheduled extract reports to produce flat files in CSV or XML format, which are placed on a secure SFTP server for downstream systems to consume on a scheduled basis.

How It Works HR administrators configure extract templates in Oracle HCM Cloud using the Data Exchange work area. Extracts run on a defined schedule, typically nightly or weekly, and place output files in a designated SFTP location. Downstream systems retrieve and process the files according to their own ingestion schedules. Data transformation is handled by the downstream system or by a lightweight script in the SFTP landing zone.

Pros No additional platform licensing required. Extract configuration is manageable by HR administrators with standard Oracle HCM skills and does not require integration developers. SFTP-based file exchange is a universally supported pattern that all target systems can accommodate. Lower technical risk during Oracle HCM quarterly upgrades because extract templates use stable HCM subject areas.

Cons Near-real-time data delivery is not possible. Downstream systems operate on stale data between extract cycles, which can cause issues for identity provisioning, payroll processing, and benefits eligibility during high-volume periods such as open enrollment or year-end. Error detection is delayed because failures surface only when downstream systems process the file, often hours after the extract completed. File-based integrations require careful schema management. Changes to extract fields or formats must be coordinated with all downstream consumers simultaneously.
Estimated Implementation Effort Low. Extract configuration and SFTP setup are well-understood patterns with abundant Oracle documentation and community resources.

Total Cost of Ownership Lowest initial cost. Higher long-term operational cost from manual coordination, delayed error detection, and file schema maintenance as both HCM and downstream systems evolve.
Comparative Summary
Real-Time Data Delivery Option 1 (OIC): Supported via event-driven triggers Option 2 (MuleSoft): Supported via event-driven or polling Option 3 (File Extract): Not supported, batch only
Implementation Complexity Option 1 (OIC): Medium Option 2 (MuleSoft): Low to Medium (if existing platform) Option 3 (File Extract): Low

Ongoing Maintenance Burden Option 1 (OIC): Low, Oracle manages platform upgrades Option 2 (MuleSoft): Medium, quarterly HCM release validation required Option 3 (File Extract): Medium to High, manual coordination per change
Governance Consolidation Option 1 (OIC): Oracle ecosystem only Option 2 (MuleSoft): Full enterprise integration consolidation Option 3 (File Extract): None, each downstream system is independent
Skill Dependency Option 1 (OIC): Oracle Integration skills Option 2 (MuleSoft): DataWeave and MuleSoft developer skills Option 3 (File Extract): Oracle HCM Extract and HR admin skills
Recommendation

For organizations that have standardized on Oracle Cloud and do not have an existing enterprise integration platform, Option 1 is recommended. Oracle Integration Cloud delivers the strongest alignment with Oracle HCM quarterly releases and provides event-driven capability that eliminates the data latency problems inherent in file-based approaches.

For organizations with an active MuleSoft investment and mature integration governance, Option 2 is recommended provided the integration team has capacity to manage quarterly HCM release validation. The reuse of existing governance infrastructure offsets the connector maintenance cost and produces a more maintainable long-term architecture.

Option 3 is appropriate as a transitional pattern during implementation phases where downstream systems are not yet ready for real-time integration, or as a permanent solution for non-time-sensitive integrations such as monthly headcount reporting or annual compensation data exports to external analysts.

Many organizations implement a combination: event-driven OIC or MuleSoft connections for payroll, identity provisioning, and benefits where latency matters, and scheduled file extracts for analytics, reporting, and audit consumers where daily batch is acceptable.
Next Steps

Confirm integration platform ownership and licensing availability with Enterprise Architecture before finalizing the approach. Identify all downstream systems and classify each by latency requirement: real-time, near-real-time, or batch. Conduct a technical spike on the highest-risk connection, typically payroll, before committing to the full integration architecture. Align the integration go-live timeline with Oracle HCM Cloud production cutover to avoid parallel maintenance of legacy and cloud integrations.
Positions vs. Jobs Management in Oracle HCM Cloud Solution Presentation and Design Decision Guide
Purpose

This document supports the design decision between Position Management and Job-based workforce modeling in Oracle HCM Cloud. It is intended for HR transformation leads, solution architects, and organizational design stakeholders evaluating how to structure their workforce hierarchy during an Oracle HCM implementation or re-implementation.
Background

Oracle HCM Cloud supports two primary models for workforce structure: a Job-based model and a Position-based model. Both models use the Job object as a foundation, but they differ significantly in how granular and controlled the assignment of workers to organizational slots is managed. A hybrid approach is also available and is increasingly common in complex global organizations.

The choice between these models has downstream implications for recruiting, headcount planning, compensation, payroll configuration, and reporting. This decision should be made early in the design phase and validated with HR, Finance, and Operations leadership before configuration begins.
Understanding Jobs in Oracle HCM Cloud

A Job in Oracle HCM Cloud represents a generic role classification that exists independently of organizational structure. Jobs carry attributes including job family, job function, grade, competency requirements, and standard working hours. The same job can be assigned to workers in any business unit, department, or location.

Jobs are managed centrally in the job catalog and are typically governed by HR or Compensation. Because jobs are not tied to a specific headcount slot or reporting line, they are well-suited for organizations with high workforce flexibility, frequent role changes, or a preference for lean organizational structures.

Key characteristics of a Job-based model include unlimited assignment of workers to the same job across the enterprise, no built-in headcount control at the role level, simplified configuration and lower administrative overhead, and faster hire and transfer transactions because position availability is not a constraint.
Understanding Positions in Oracle HCM Cloud

A Position in Oracle HCM Cloud represents a specific, budgeted slot within a department and location. Each position is associated with a job but adds specificity through its placement in the organizational hierarchy. A position carries its own attributes including FTE, grade, probation period, shift, and reporting relationships.

Positions enable headcount control because a position can be configured to allow only one incumbent, multiple incumbents up to an FTE cap, or an unlimited number of incumbents. When a worker is terminated, the position remains in the organizational structure and can be refilled, which preserves institutional knowledge about role requirements and compensation benchmarks.

Key characteristics of a Position-based model include explicit headcount governance at the role level, a persistent organizational structure that survives workforce changes, tighter integration with Oracle Recruiting for requisition creation tied to open positions, more complex configuration and higher administrative overhead to maintain the position inventory, and stronger support for regulated industries and public sector organizations where headcount authorization is required.
Comparing the Two Models

Headcount Control In a Job-based model, headcount is managed through reporting and budgeting tools outside the core HR record. Finance and HR must reconcile headcount independently. In a Position-based model, headcount is governed directly in the workforce structure. The system enforces FTE limits and can prevent overhiring without a position override.

Recruiting Integration Oracle Recruiting Cloud creates job requisitions that link to either a job or a position. In a Position-based model, the requisition inherits position attributes including department, grade, and hiring manager, reducing data entry errors and ensuring consistency between the open role and the eventual hire record. In a Job-based model, recruiters enter these attributes manually for each requisition.

Organizational Change Management When departments are reorganized, a Job-based model requires updating worker assignment records to reflect new reporting lines and cost centers. A Position-based model allows the positions themselves to be moved within the hierarchy, with incumbent assignments following automatically. For large-scale reorganizations this can significantly reduce the volume of individual assignment changes.

Compensation and Grade Enforcement Both models support grade ladders and compensation bands, but the enforcement mechanism differs. In a Position-based model, grade can be enforced at the position level, restricting the grades available for any worker assigned to that position. In a Job-based model, grade enforcement is applied at the job level and applies uniformly across all workers in that job regardless of organizational context.

Reporting and Analytics Position-based models produce cleaner headcount and vacancy reporting because the organizational structure is explicit. Vacancy reports, span-of-control analysis, and FTE utilization reports are more straightforward to build and interpret. Job-based models require additional reporting logic to infer organizational intent from assignment data.
When to Choose a Job-Based Model

Organizations that benefit most from a Job-based model typically share several characteristics. The workforce is highly dynamic with frequent role changes and project-based assignments. Organizational structures are flat or frequently reorganized. HR administrative capacity is limited and maintaining a position inventory is not feasible. The organization operates in a single country or a small number of similar legislative environments. Speed and simplicity of hire transactions is prioritized over headcount governance.
When to Choose a Position-Based Model

Organizations that benefit most from a Position-based model typically include those that require strict headcount authorization, such as public sector, healthcare, financial services, and defense contractors. The workforce structure is relatively stable with well-defined roles and reporting relationships. The organization uses Oracle Recruiting Cloud and wants to leverage position-driven requisition creation. Finance and HR have agreed to manage headcount through the HR system of record rather than through external budget tools. The organization operates across multiple countries with different legislative requirements that benefit from position-level configuration.
The Hybrid Approach

Many enterprise organizations adopt a hybrid model in which certain business units or legal employers use Position Management while others operate on a Job-based model. Oracle HCM Cloud supports this at the enterprise level through the Enable Position Management option at the legal employer or business unit level.

Common hybrid patterns include using Position Management for headquarters and regulated entities while using a Job-based model for field operations and contract workers, applying Position Management to permanent employees and a Job-based model to contingent and temporary workers, and enabling Position Management only for business units that have completed organizational design work, with plans to extend to remaining units in subsequent phases.

The hybrid approach introduces complexity in cross-unit transfers and headcount reporting. Organizations adopting a hybrid model should define clear governance rules for how workers moving between position-managed and job-managed units are handled.
Implementation Recommendations
Regardless of which model is selected, the following practices support a successful implementation.

Define the position or job governance model before configuration begins. Decide who owns job catalog maintenance, who approves position creation, and how grade ladders are managed across business units.

Cleanse source data before migration. Job titles, grade codes, and department hierarchies sourced from legacy systems often contain inconsistencies that become structural defects in Oracle HCM Cloud if not resolved before load.

Conduct a headcount reconciliation between the current HR system and the proposed Oracle structure before go-live. Discrepancies between active headcount and position inventory are one of the most common causes of delayed go-live in Oracle HCM implementations.

Involve Finance in the design session. The connection between position FTE, compensation budget, and Oracle Planning and Budgeting Cloud Services is strongest when Finance and HR align on the model early.

Plan for ongoing governance. Position inventories require regular review to retire vacant positions, update grade ladders, and reflect organizational changes. Without a defined governance cadence, position inventories become stale and undermine the value of the Position-based model.

Chart Spec Catalog
$schema doc: Chart Spec Catalog — Auto PPT v1

$description: Canonical collection of chart payloads for the asset stage. Each entry is a complete chart_spec dict that can be passed directly to render_chart_to_png() (supported specs) or used as a contract target for renderer expansion (planned specs). See $field_reference for the full intended schema.
$field reference

Field | Value chart_type | string — one of the values listed in $supported_chart_types or $planned_chart_types title | string — slide-level chart title rendered above the plot x_label | string | null — axis label for the x-axis (omit for pie/donut/kpi_tile) y_label | string | null — axis label for the y-axis (omit for pie/donut/kpi_tile) accent_color | hex string — primary series color; default #0A84FF palette | list[hex] — ordered color list for multi-series or segmented charts data | list[{label, value}] — flat data shape consumed by current renderer for single-series charts series | list[{name, data: list[{label, value}]}] — multi-series shape for stacked/grouped/multi-line/combo charts categories | list[string] — explicit x-axis category order; inferred from data[].label when absent show_data_labels | bool — render value labels directly on bars/points/segments show_legend | bool — render a legend; always false for single-series charts aspect_ratio | float — target width/height for the output PNG; used by asset resolver for slot fit/fill intended_template_keys | list[string] — template registry keys this chart is designed to fill renderer_status | supported | planned — whether
chart_renderer.py currently handles this chart_type
$supported chart types
bar
line
pie
scatter
$planned chart types
stacked_bar
grouped_bar
area
multi_line
donut
waterfall
horizontal_bar
kpi_tile
combo_bar_line

Deterministic Geometry and Template Registry Architecture for Automated Presentation GenerationThe engineering of modern automated presentation systems has moved beyond the stochastic generation of slide content toward a deterministic architecture where design logic is codified into strict geometric registries. This evolution is driven by the necessity for professional-grade output that adheres to brand standards and corporate communication norms, a task that requires a profound understanding of the relationship between declarative content representations and the physical coordinate space of the digital canvas. When implementing such a system using Codex or similar agentic architectures, the most critical component is the layout resolver and its associated template registry, often implemented as a layout/templates.py module.

This registry acts as the source of truth, translating abstract slide intents into absolute inch-based coordinates that govern the placement of textboxes, images, tables, and charts.The Convergence of Design Logic and Software OrchestrationThe transition from a "presentation generator" to a "presentation architect" requires a shift in how layout is conceptualized. Traditional AI slide tools often struggle with visual consistency because they attempt to generate layout on the fly, leading to overlapping elements, inconsistent margins, and typographic "wall of text" failures. A more robust approach, visible in the architectures of industry leaders such as Microsoft Copilot, Gamma, and Beautiful.ai, involves a hybrid model. In this model, the large language model (LLM) is responsible for content planning and "filling" pre-defined slots, while a deterministic layout service handles the physical positioning and sizing of elements.This deterministic service relies on a SlideSpec—a stable intermediate representation that defines what a slide should say without dictating its final appearance.

The layout resolver then consumes this SlideSpec and maps its content blocks to specific slots within a chosen template. This process is governed by a coordinate system based on real-world measurements, typically inches, to maintain compatibility with standard Office Open XML (OOXML) rendering libraries like python-pptx and PptxGenJS. For professional peers in the field of computational design, this represents a separation of concerns that ensures every slide produced is visually balanced, brand-compliant, and optimized for its intended display environment.The Physical Canvas: Mathematical Foundations of the 16:9 SlideThe standard for modern digital presentations is the 16:9 widescreen aspect ratio. In the context of Microsoft PowerPoint, this defaults to a physical size of $13.333$ inches in width and $7.5$ inches in height. This rectangular shape is designed to fill the viewports of contemporary laptops, tablets, and projectors perfectly, providing approximately $100$ square inches of total design area.

Understanding these dimensions is foundational for setting up a template registry, as every coordinate defined must exist within these boundaries to avoid "out-of-bounds" errors during rendering.When calculating the available space for content, one must account for the "safe area" defined by margins. Professional slide design typically follows a "Normal Margin" standard, which provides a $0.75$-inch buffer on all four sides. This leaves a primary content area of $11.833$ inches by $6.0$ inches. Within this area, a sophisticated grid system is employed to organize information.

A 12-column grid is particularly effective due to its high divisibility, allowing for layouts of 2, 3, 4, or 6 columns with ease.Dimension MetricInch ValueMathematical RationaleSlide Width13.333Standard 16:9 Aspect Ratio Slide Height7.5Standard 16:9 Aspect Ratio Top Margin0.75Business Standard header clearance Bottom Margin0.75Footer and citation clearance Content Width11.833$13.333 - (0.75 \times 2)$ Content Height6.0$7.5 - (0.75 \times 2)$ Column Gutter0.20Standard separation for 12-col layout Row Gutter0.25Standard separation for vertical stacks Theoretical Framework of Grid-Based Slide LayoutsThe implementation of a template registry requires more than just raw numbers; it requires a design philosophy rooted in vertical rhythm and horizontal balance. A grid system creates an invisible framework that guides the viewer's eye through the slide content. In a 12-column system, each column has a width of approximately $0.80$ inches, separated by $0.20$-inch gutters.

This granularity allows for precise alignment of text and visuals, ensuring that elements like headlines and callouts are not placed arbitrarily but follow a logical structure.Vertical offsets, or y-coordinates, are particularly important for maintaining consistency across a deck. If the headline of slide 2 is at $Y=0.75$ and the headline of slide 3 is at $Y=1.0$, the transition between them will appear "jumpy" to the audience. A deterministic registry prevents this by fixing the header slot height and vertical position for all standard templates. Furthermore, the height of slots must be calculated based on the typographic hierarchy. A headline using 36-point font (which is $0.5$ inches tall) requires a slot height of at least $0.75$ to $1.0$ inch to allow for line spacing and the visual weight of the text.The Ingestion and Planning Pipeline: Precursors to Layout ResolutionBefore a template can be selected and populated, the system must parse and normalize the input data. This process, known as ingestion, transforms raw PDF, Word, or HTML files into a structured Knowledge Corpus.

Using libraries such as pdfplumber for text extraction and Tesseract for OCR, the system partitions documents into typed elements like titles, list items, and tables. Each element is assigned a unique ID and stored with metadata that preserves its provenance.The planning service then utilizes this knowledge through a multi-step prompt chain. It first defines the "Brief"—the goals and constraints of the presentation—before generating a slide-by-slide outline. For each slide intent, the system retrieves relevant content chunks from a vector index (e.g., FAISS or Qdrant) using Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The final step of this planning stage is the generation of a SlideSpec, which identifies the template_key for each slide and maps specific content blocks to the available slots. This SlideSpec is what the layout service ultimately resolves into physical geometry.The Layout Service and the Registry PatternThe Layout Service acts as the bridge between design intent and physical reality. Its primary function is to translate the logical slot names in the SlideSpec (e.g., headline, col_left) into the absolute coordinates $(x, y, w, h)$ required by the PPTX renderer.

This is achieved through the registry pattern, where the layout/templates.py file contains a dictionary of template definitions. Each entry in the registry defines the number of slots, their types (textbox, image, chart), and their precise geometric boundaries on the $13.33 \times 7.5$ canvas.A critical feature of the layout service is its ability to enforce constraints. It ensures that no element exceeds the slide boundary and that there is no overlap between elements. These rules are not merely suggestions but hard constraints that trigger a redesign if violated. This level of control allows for the production of slides that match the quality of hand-designed decks while maintaining the efficiency of automation.Detailed Geometry Definitions for the Canonical Template LibraryThe following sections detail the geometry for the ten canonical templates required for a professional automated presentation system. Each template is designed for a specific communicative purpose, utilizing a consistent grid and margin system.1. The title.hero Template: Establishing Initial AuthorityThe title slide is the most visually distinct template in any registry.

Its purpose is to command attention and establish the brand identity of the presentation. Unlike standard content slides, it often breaks the standard margin to create a sense of scale. The title.hero template focuses on a central vertical axis, with the headline and subtitle placed in the upper two-thirds of the slide, and brand assets anchored at the bottom.Slot IDKindX (in)Y (in)W (in)H (in)Design Rationaleheadlinetextbox1.502.5010.331.25Large 44pt title, centered horizontally subtitletextbox2.004.009.330.75Supporting text, 24pt, tight to headline presentertextbox0.756.505.000.40Left-aligned metadata for presenter datetextbox0.756.905.000.40Anchored below presenter info logoimage11.506.501.080.50Bottom-right brand signature The vertical placement of the headline at $Y=2.50$ ensures that it rests on the "visual center" of the slide, which is slightly above the mathematical center. This creates a more balanced and professional feel. The large width of the headline slot ($10.33$ inches) allows for long, descriptive titles without immediate line wrapping.2.

The agenda.list Template: Navigating the NarrativeThe agenda.list template is an organizational tool that provides the audience with a roadmap for the presentation. It requires a clear typographic hierarchy, where the section numbers or bullets are clearly separated from the descriptive text. The geometry follows the standard "Business Normal" margins to ensure content is easily readable in both digital and projected formats.Slot IDKindX (in)Y (in)W (in)H (in)Design Rationaleheadlinetextbox0.750.7511.830.75Global standard header slot agenda_bodybullets1.501.8010.334.80Indented to create clear list area progress_barshape0.007.3513.330.15Full-bleed bar for navigation tracking By indenting the agenda_body to $X=1.50$, the system creates an "inner margin" that adds visual breathing room, a technique essential for lists that contain complex information. The inclusion of a full-bleed progress_bar slot allows the agent to update the visual state of the deck as it progresses through sections.3. The section.header Template: Signaling Structural ShiftsSection headers act as mental palette cleansers, signaling the end of one topic and the beginning of another.

The design for section.header is often minimal, relying on a large headline and a secondary "tagline" slot to frame the upcoming content. To differentiate from title slides, these are often rendered with a distinct accent background color defined in the StyleTokens.Slot IDKindX (in)Y (in)W (in)H (in)Design Rationaleheadlinetextbox1.003.0011.331.25Centered vertically for max impact taglinetextbox1.004.3011.330.60Supports the headline message footer_infotextbox0.756.8011.830.30Optional page numbering or section ID The spacing between the headline and the tagline is $0.05$ inches ($4.30 - (3.00 + 1.25)$), which maintains a tight visual grouping, ensuring the audience reads them as a single cohesive message.4. The content.1col Template: The Core Information VehicleThis template is the standard workhorse for any presentation. It is used for detailed narrative, bullets, and single-point insights. The system applies a strict text density rule here, limiting body text to approximately 40 words to ensure legibility from a distance.

The geometry follows a top-down information hierarchy, anchored by a standard header.Slot IDKindX (in)Y (in)W (in)H (in)Design Rationaleheadlinetextbox0.750.7511.830.75Standard header slot body_textbullets0.751.7511.834.50Primary content container takeawayshape0.756.4011.830.80Key takeaway footer box The takeaway slot at the bottom is strategically placed to capture the "Z-pattern" of eye movement, where the viewer ends their scan of the slide at the bottom edge. $11.83$), allowing for additional columns or longer lines of text.Typographic Constraints and Vertical RhythmThe geometric precision of the template registry is ineffective if it is not paired with rigorous typographic rules. In the field of automated slide generation, typography is not just an aesthetic choice but a geometric constraint that impacts layout stability. For business presentations, sans-serif fonts such as Aptos, Segoe UI, or Arial are standard because they maintain clarity across various screen resolutions.The relationship between font size and slot height is the primary cause of overflow errors. A title slot of $0.75$ inches height is perfectly suited for a $36$-point headline, which occupies $0.5$ inches of vertical space, leaving $0.125$ inches of padding on both the top and bottom. If the LLM generates a headline that is too long, the system must either wrap the text (increasing the vertical footprint) or reduce the font size.

Professional systems typically set a "minimum readable size" for body text (usually $18\text{pt}$ to $20\text{pt}$), beyond which the layout service must flag an overflow violation.Element TypeTarget Font SizeMin Font SizeSlot ImpactSlide Title32pt - 36pt28pt$H=0.75"$ or $H=1.00"$ Body Text20pt - 24pt18pt$H$ varies by bullet count Captions14pt - 16pt12pt$H=0.30"$ KPI Metrics48pt+36pt$H=2.00"+$ Asset Integration and Geometric ScalingBeyond text, the layout service must manage visual assets such as images and icons. The registry defines "image slots" that specify the intended aspect ratio. For example, a $50/50$ content split provides an image area of $5.82 \times 5.00$ inches ($1.16:1$ ratio). If the retrieved image does not match this ratio, the renderer must implement a "fill" or "fit" policy to avoid distortion.A common failure mode in agentic slide systems is the use of authenticated or short-lived URLs for images. As noted in Gamma and Canva API documentation, such URLs can expire within 24 hours, leading to broken assets in the final PPTX. To mitigate this, the Asset Service must download and cache all external assets locally at the time of layout resolution.

These assets are then embedded directly into the OOXML package as local parts, ensuring that the presentation remains functional even if the source URL is no longer available.Quality Assurance and Deterministic Validation GatesThe final stage before the PPTX is delivered to the user is the Quality Assurance (QA) check. This stage is responsible for auditing the ResolvedLayout against the physical constraints of the slide. The primary checks are for element overlap, slide boundary overflow, and citation coverage.The overlap check is mathematically defined as the intersection of two rectangles $R_a$ and $R_b$. If the ratio of the intersection area to the total slide area exceeds $0.01$, the slide is flagged. This is a critical gate that ensures professional polish. Similarly, the citation coverage check verifies that every non-trivial factual claim (defined during the parsing stage) is linked to a citation block in the final layout. This maintains the provenance and trust chain essential for enterprise-grade automation.The Future of Agentic Slide DesignThe transition to a deterministic, registry-based architecture represents a maturation of AI-driven presentation systems.

By moving from free-form generation to a model where an agent fills predefined geometric slots, developers can ensure that the output is consistently professional, readable, and compliant with corporate standards. The coordinates provided in this report for the ten canonical templates serve as the necessary foundation for such a system, enabling the layout resolver to operate with mathematical precision on the standard 16:9 canvas.As these systems continue to evolve, the integration of real-time data connectors and dynamic charting will further increase the complexity of layout resolution. However, the core principle remains the same: visual quality is achieved through the separation of content intent and physical geometry. By defining these templates thoughtfully in layout/templates.py, developers empower their systems to produce work that is indistinguishable from that of a professional human designer, all while leveraging the immense efficiency of agentic workflows.
